title: The Crowening

jokes.text: Johnny fumbles with the phone trying to figure out how to put Moira on speaker while the family shouts at him
jokes.setup: Johnny announces Moira is on the phone and calls for everyone to keep her spirits high
jokes.punchline: He can't figure out how to use the speaker function while everyone shouts 'Answer the phone!' and 'Speaker, speaker!' at him
jokes.explanation: Classic fish-out-of-water tech confusion for a formerly wealthy patriarch now fumbling with a motel phone. The chaos is earned but not a particularly novel gag. Works because of the family dynamic energy.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Okay, family, we need to present a united front here. When I call, I need you all to sound supportive and successful.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, how do I unmute? I've unmuted myself.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, you're still on mute.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, is my camera on? Can everyone see me?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I can't see anyone. Why is it so dark?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Because you put your phone in your purse.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Okay, everyone just— Can everyone hear me? Hello?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: John, I don't think I'm plugged in.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, you have a cell phone.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'She's not my mother.'
jokes.setup: Johnny says 'Kids, say hi to your mother' and everyone mumbles hello
jokes.punchline: Stevie, who is the motel employee, dryly clarifies she is not part of this family
jokes.explanation: Perfectly economical deadpan. Stevie's dry detachment is her core character trait and this four-word line nails it. The joke works because Johnny has inadvertently folded Stevie into the family, which she refuses. Character-specific and well-timed.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: There's such sweetness in the air on set today.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It's literally because of the baklava factory next door.

jokes.text: Moira mistakes David's voice for Alexis's and asks if Alexis has strep again
jokes.setup: Johnny tells the kids to say hi to their mother over the phone
jokes.punchline: Moira hears 'Hey' and immediately assumes it's Alexis with strep — implying David sounds sickly or weak-voiced enough to be confused for a sick teenage girl
jokes.explanation: Elegant economy — one line simultaneously insults David, reveals Moira's distracted parenting, and stays completely in character for all three. The misidentification gag lands efficiently.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, whose voices are these?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: They're our children, Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, they sound nothing like I remember.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's because they're literally standing right here in front of you.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Oh Alexis, do you have strep again?' / 'That was David!'
jokes.setup: Moira hears voices on the call and tries to identify her children
jokes.punchline: She mistakes David's voice for Alexis having strep throat — implying David sounds sick or whiny by default
jokes.explanation: Moira's oblivious casual cruelty toward her children is a signature comedic mode. The joke lands because it simultaneously insults David and reveals Moira's distracted self-absorption. The strep detail is a funny specific choice.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David? Is that you?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, Mom, it's me.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh my God, Alexis, your strep throat sounds absolutely dreadful.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, it's David.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, yes, I know, sweetie. Now go gargle with salt water.

jokes.text: Moira: 'David! Oh, David, how's the store? Any new customers, or is it still friendless?'
jokes.setup: Moira warmly greets David and asks about the store
jokes.punchline: Her affectionate greeting immediately pivots to 'or is it still friendless?' — a casual brutal assessment delivered with zero self-awareness
jokes.explanation: This is quintessential Moira — warmth and casual devastation in the same breath, with no awareness that 'friendless' is a cutting word. The structure is tight: affectionate opening, comma, brutal pivot. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: How is David's business doing?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's doing well. We're quite busy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: That's wonderful. And how would you describe the atmosphere of the store?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's friendless.

jokes.text: Moira: 'And John, I know how disoriented you get in my absence. Don't forget I left you that Sudoku book.'
jokes.setup: Moira checks in on the family from set
jokes.punchline: She treats her adult husband like a child who needs a puzzle book to cope without her — with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: The Sudoku book as a coping mechanism for an otherwise competent man is a funny specific detail. Moira's condescension is loving and oblivious simultaneously — classic character work.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I love you so much, you know that? You're my everything. I don't know what I'd do without you. You make every day special, and I just want you to know how much you mean to—

jokes.text: Moira: 'Oh John, there's a sweetness in the air, and I'm not just saying that because we shoot next to a baklava factory.'
jokes.setup: Moira describes the poetic ambiance of being back on a film set
jokes.punchline: She anchors her romantic description in the extremely mundane and specific detail of an adjacent baklava factory
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's better lines. The structure is perfect: a sweeping poetic claim immediately undercut by a hyper-specific mundane reality. 'Baklava factory' is an inspired detail — specific enough to be absurd, plausible enough to land. Moira's delivery would make this even funnier — she treats it as an important clarification rather than a deflation.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Dad, why would you even say that out loud?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's genuinely one of the worst things I've ever heard come out of a human mouth.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Like, worse than when you tried to return those pants without a receipt.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That was humiliating, and this is somehow worse.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I was simply trying to—

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No. There's no explanation that makes this better.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Well, sounds like your kind of people.' (referring to the 'up-and-coming pretty young thing' director)
jokes.setup: Moira excitedly describes the director as 'some up-and-coming pretty young thing out of New York'
jokes.punchline: Johnny dryly notes this is exactly the type of person Moira would be drawn to — the implication being this is a mild dig at her vanity and social climbing
jokes.explanation: A gentle, knowing jab from Johnny about Moira's personality. Works but is understated to the point of mild. Johnny's affectionate tolerance is his character mode, so this fits, but it doesn't reach for anything surprising.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Stevie, I need your honest opinion on something.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: That's never good.

jokes.text: Johnny keeps talking after Moira has already hung up: 'Lots to do, but wishing you an exciting...' — then silence, then 'Okay, yep. Miss you, too.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is in the middle of a warm farewell speech to Moira
jokes.punchline: She's already hung up mid-sentence, leaving him talking to dead air and then awkwardly pretending she said something back
jokes.explanation: A classic comedic beat — the oblivious hang-up. Works well here because it humanizes Johnny's devotion (he was really into that goodbye) and gently reveals Moira's self-absorption. 'Miss you, too' to silence is the nice finishing touch.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm worried about you and Patrick. I found your score on a quiz from an old magazine.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What quiz?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: "How compatible are you with your partner?" You got a 12.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Out of what?

jokes.text: Johnny: 'You better remember which nails you pulled those wigs from, because your mother keeps a spreadsheet.'
jokes.setup: The kids are messing with Moira's wigs while she's away
jokes.punchline: Johnny warns them Moira tracks her wig organization obsessively via spreadsheet — a very specific, believable detail about Moira's character
jokes.explanation: The wig spreadsheet is an excellent Moira-specific detail. It tells you everything about her character — theatrical, controlling, precise about her performance accessories — in one image. Good economy and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's not 'M'Lady Magazine,' it's 'Everyday Nouveau Riche.' There's a difference.

jokes.text: David: 'Your natural deodorant isn't working.' (said immediately when Alexis calls him over privately)
jokes.setup: Alexis calls David over, saying 'I have a concern'
jokes.punchline: David immediately assumes the concern is about his natural deodorant — an overshare that reveals his own anxieties while deflecting
jokes.explanation: David's preemptive self-consciousness and the specificity of 'natural deodorant' is a sharp character beat. It's funny because he's both defensive and oversharing, and it completely wrong-foots the scene's expected direction. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: This quiz is garbage.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: David, you took it seventeen times.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Because it's garbage.

jokes.text: Alexis reveals she was worried because of David's score on 'How Electric is your Relationship' quiz from M'Lady Magazine, summer 1991
jokes.setup: Alexis says she got scared and wanted to make sure David and Patrick were okay
jokes.punchline: The basis of her concern is a relationship quiz from a magazine from 1991 — the specificity of 'summer 1991' is the comedic detail that elevates this
jokes.explanation: The joke is layered: a vintage magazine quiz as the basis of serious relationship concern, the portentous delivery, and the specificity of 'summer 1991.' The name 'M'Lady Magazine' is also a good absurdist flourish.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Your relationship is snooze-town.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Ted is not uptight. He's... conscientious.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: He color-codes his sock drawer.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's called being organized.

jokes.text: David: 'Okay, first of all, it's Major Lady Magazine' — correcting the magazine name with complete seriousness
jokes.setup: Alexis calls it 'M'Lady Magazine'
jokes.punchline: David's priority in this humiliating moment is correcting the magazine's name — which is equally ridiculous — with total seriousness
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed character beat. The fact that David has been caught taking a trashy quiz AND his instinct is to correct the magazine's name (which is equally embarrassing) perfectly encapsulates his defensive snobbery. The deadpan correction is funnier than any bluster could be.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, actually, we got a 94% on our couple's compatibility quiz.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So...

jokes.text: Alexis: 'This garbage quiz that it looks like you took more than once.'
jokes.setup: David dismisses the quiz as garbage
jokes.punchline: Alexis agrees — and notes that he clearly took the 'garbage' quiz multiple times anyway
jokes.explanation: Tight comedic structure. Alexis lets David hang himself — she agrees it's garbage, then twists the knife by pointing out the evidence of obsessive retaking. The economy is excellent; the punchline is short and lands cleanly.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: We have incredible chemistry. Like, the electricity between us is insane.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, well, you've always been drawn to literal electricity. Remember when you stuck a fork in the toaster? And the hairdryer incident?

jokes.text: David: 'Yeah, because it's rigged. You could take it 1000 times you'd still get the same outcome.'
jokes.setup: Alexis points out he took the quiz more than once
jokes.punchline: David's defense is that he took it many times to prove it was rigged — which only proves how invested he was in the results
jokes.explanation: David's defensive logic is so transparently self-defeating that it's funny. The irony is built into his argument — he's essentially admitting he kept failing the quiz. Good character-specific escalation.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It happened twice.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Twice?!

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, that makes it worse.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'You're dating a vet who irons his polo shirts.'
jokes.setup: Alexis describes David and Patrick's settled relationship as 'nine to five life' and 'snooze-town'
jokes.punchline: She crystallizes it with the image of Ted ironing polo shirts — a devastatingly specific picture of comfortable suburban contentment
jokes.explanation: A sharp, economical image. 'A vet who irons his polo shirts' is a perfect encapsulation of Ted's personality — wholesome, responsible, slightly square. The specific detail of the polo shirt ironing is inspired; it's funny because it's so accurate and so mild.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: We could do a Friday adventure date!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: An adventure date? That sounds like code for 'let's go somewhere uncomfortable and I'll pretend to enjoy it while secretly resenting you.'

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Um, Ted and I got "Electrical Storm." So.'
jokes.setup: David has mocked Alexis's relationship descriptor
jokes.punchline: Alexis drops that she and Ted scored the top tier result on the very quiz she's been using to lecture David, delivered with supreme self-satisfaction and a mic-drop 'So.'
jokes.explanation: The sentence structure here is immaculate — the setup is the implication that Alexis scored well, and the 'So.' is a perfect mic-drop. The economy is exceptional: four words plus a trailing 'So' that carries enormous smugness. Alexis's personality is entirely in that delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Don't you have some boring work to do?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's not boring. I'm doing a very important inventory.

jokes.text: David: 'Did you plug your hairdryer in with wet hands again?'
jokes.setup: David is stunned that Alexis scored 'Electrical Storm' and demands to know what she did to get it
jokes.punchline: He suggests the only explanation is that she nearly electrocuted herself with a hairdryer — which turns out to be a reference to something that actually happened
jokes.explanation: The hairdryer wet-hands joke works on two levels: David's incredulous dismissal and the implication that this is a real thing Alexis has done. The callback-within-the-scene makes it richer.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: So you got 'Competitive Ballroom Dancer'?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I would rather eat glass.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, the quiz thinks you'd rather eat glass in a sequined unitard.

jokes.text: David: '"Adventure dates" sound like something a marriage counselor would prescribe as a last resort.'
jokes.setup: Alexis describes how she and Ted take Friday afternoon 'adventure dates' to keep the sparks flying
jokes.punchline: David reframes their romantic gesture as desperate therapeutic intervention
jokes.explanation: Sharp observational humor with strong character voice. David's cynicism reframes something genuinely sweet as clinical desperation — the 'last resort' detail is what makes it land. This feels true to how David defends his own insecurities by attacking others.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, this is marvelous. I feel a kinship with these magnificent creatures already.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: CAW! CAW! One must embrace the character, darling. The method is everything.

jokes.text: David agrees to come to the park, then: 'It's merchandising day, actually.' (when Alexis suggests they have more exciting things to do like put price tags on things)
jokes.setup: Alexis sarcastically suggests they probably have exciting things to do like 'put price tags on things'
jokes.punchline: David, with complete dignity, clarifies it is in fact 'merchandising day' — accidentally validating her mockery
jokes.explanation: David's dignity in the face of his own self-parody is a rich seam of character comedy. The correction to 'merchandising day' is funnier than anything else he could have said because it proves Alexis's point while he thinks he's making his own.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I've given my character a deeply traumatic past. She was abandoned by her murder of crows as a chick, forcing her to fend for herself in a harsh urban landscape.

dialogue.speaker: Director
dialogue.line: It's a commercial for a local pest control company.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, and she's a crow with layers.

jokes.text: David: 'Mm! Eat glass.' (in response to Alexis's parting concern)
jokes.setup: Alexis offers a parting shot about David's relationship potentially being a 'Total Power Failure'
jokes.punchline: David's response to this concern-troll is the entirely disproportionate 'Eat glass'
jokes.explanation: 'Eat glass' is a wildly specific and funny insult — violent enough to be surprising, vague enough to be absurd. The 'Mm!' before it, as if he's considering a pleasanter response before discarding it, makes it perfect. This is peak David Rose and extremely quotable. The economy is near-perfect: two words that do enormous character work.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, you know, I've worked on productions with far more limited resources. I once did a student film about a crow that was trying to warn a family about an impending winter. It was basically The Shining, but with poultry.

jokes.text: Moira enters the film set with theatrical proclamation: 'Caw! Caw! My teamster brethren!'
jokes.setup: Moira arrives on set for her crow movie
jokes.punchline: She greets the crew by cawing like a crow and calling them her 'teamster brethren' — fully committing to the crow persona before cameras are even rolling
jokes.explanation: Moira treating a B-movie crow fantasy as serious artistic work she is already deeply inhabiting is quintessential character comedy. 'My teamster brethren' is the funniest part — the mix of crow performance and labor union language is delightfully absurd. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Director
dialogue.line: So, what do you think of the script?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It's terrible. Absolutely terrible. But I'm committed to it.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Doctor Clara Beatrice Mandrake at your service. Middle name wasn't scripted, but I've done my homework.'
jokes.setup: Moira introduces herself to director Blaire on set
jokes.punchline: She has invented a middle name for her character and treats this unsolicited deep-dive as professional diligence
jokes.explanation: Excellent Moira moment. She's playing a character in a B-movie about mutant crows, and she has given that character an un-scripted middle name as part of her 'homework.' The gap between the movie's quality and her Method preparation is the joke — and it's very specific to who Moira is.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: So the director wants me to play a woman who discovers her husband has been replaced by an alien, and she has to seduce the alien to save the world.

dialogue.speaker: Director
dialogue.line: It's not about seduction, Moira. It's about the primal feminine energy reclaiming dominion over the masculine Other. It's Sartre meets Hitchcock.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It's a B-movie where I take my clothes off.

dialogue.speaker: Director
dialogue.line: Exactly. The disrobing is a metaphor for the shedding of societal constraints and the embrace of one's true cosmic self.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Break me down just to build me up again! Like Stan Kubrick did to Shelley.'
jokes.setup: Director Blaire calls Moira 'Rose' and seems disinterested, which Moira interprets as a power game
jokes.punchline: She compares this minor professional slight to Stanley Kubrick's famously brutal treatment of Shelley Duvall on The Shining — calling him 'Stan Kubrick' as if they're on a first-name basis
jokes.explanation: Two jokes in one: Moira's grandiose self-comparison to Shelley Duvall (implying she is a serious auteur-tormented actress) and the casual 'Stan Kubrick' — name-dropping on first-name terms as if she and Stanley Kubrick were close. Her delusion is consistently funny.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, that's nice. I once ran into Portia de Rossi at a Bora Bora resort, and let me tell you, she was not as charming in person. Actually, that wasn't Portia de Rossi, that was just a woman named Patricia who worked at the concierge desk.

jokes.text: Moira: 'I'm kidding. The script's a total dumpster fire, of course.'
jokes.setup: Moira approaches the director saying she has 'just a few character adjustments' to the script, then says she's kidding
jokes.punchline: She immediately reverses — she was kidding about the minor adjustments, because the script is actually a total disaster
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection structure. The fake-out of 'I'm kidding' followed by an escalation rather than a retreat is a solid comedic construction. Moira's honesty is more brutal than her original implied complaint.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Johnny's been doing my job for me. I don't know what he's thinking.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Next thing I know, he'll be playing Scrabble with my wife.

jokes.text: Blaire: 'If the script were garbage, I don't believe we'd have signed on.' / Moira: 'It's an apocalyptic fantasy about mutant crows. I think we all know what we're making here.'
jokes.setup: Blaire defends the script's quality
jokes.punchline: Moira cuts through the pretension by simply describing the literal premise — 'mutant crows' as the punchline to any high-minded defense
jokes.explanation: Strong comedic structure. The joke hinges on the gap between how people in showbiz talk about projects and the actual content of those projects. 'Mutant crows' as the deflating fact that ends the argument is well-constructed. 'I think we all know what we're making here' is a great line — weary, self-aware, precise.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You think you're so smart, don't you, Johnny? Moving in, taking over my town, my business. Pretty soon you'll be wearing my clothes, sleeping in my bed.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, I have no interest in any of those things.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: That's what they all say! But then one day you wake up and you're not Roland Schitt anymore. You're just... some guy. Some guy who looks like me, talks like me, but ISN'T me. Who am I, Johnny? WHO AM I?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You're Roland Schitt. The mayor.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Am I though? AM I?

jokes.text: Blaire: 'A trip to Bora Bora.' (when Moira asks what the project means to Blaire)
jokes.setup: Moira presses Blaire: 'And what exactly is this to you, then?' — implying Blaire should have artistic investment
jokes.punchline: Blaire drops all pretense: the entire project is a paycheck vacation fund
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedic economy — the gap between what Moira asked and the three-word answer is the entire joke. 'A trip to Bora Bora' is funnier than any elaborate explanation could be. The directness is shocking and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I'm the gutter guy! I've been the gutter guy for twenty years!

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You can't just take that away from me!

jokes.text: Blaire: 'Okay, let's just cash our paycheques, and pray no one ever sees this, yeah?'
jokes.setup: Moira has been trying to elevate the artistic conversation about their mutant crow project
jokes.punchline: Blaire cuts the pretension entirely — the plan is to get paid and hope no one watches
jokes.explanation: A functional cynical punchline to the Moira/Blaire scene. It's not the most original joke but it's well-placed and lands because of the contrast with Moira's artistic pretension. The 'pray no one ever sees this' is a nice self-deprecating admission about B-movies.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Johnny, I'm telling you, you're taking over my life. First it was the business, now it's my doctor's appointments.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, I'm simply trying to help you stay organized.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Organized? You scheduled me for a colonoscopy without asking me!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's medically necessary, Roland.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: That's not the point, Johnny. That's a very intimate procedure. The only thing that should be going up there is my own dignity!

jokes.text: Roland: 'I mean, come on, what's next? I go home and he's playing strip backgammon with my wife?'
jokes.setup: Roland escalates his complaints about Johnny doing all his jobs
jokes.punchline: His anxiety about job-replacement becomes genuinely unhinged when he imagines Johnny playing strip backgammon with Jocelyn — an extremely specific, bizarre scenario
jokes.explanation: The jump to strip backgammon is a great comedic escalation. The specificity of backgammon (not poker, not chess — backgammon) and the strip version makes it wonderfully weird. Roland's paranoid logic is perfectly calibrated to his character.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, Johnny, these moles are suspicious. I need a professional opinion.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, I'm a business man, not a dermatologist.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You're the most trustworthy person in this town. If anyone's going to check my moles, it's you.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That is... not how medicine works.

jokes.text: Roland sees Johnny in a work shirt: 'Oh, look! This guy's wearing a work shirt. He's even copping my look now!'
jokes.setup: Roland's growing paranoia that Johnny is taking over his life
jokes.punchline: Johnny is wearing a work shirt, which Roland interprets as identity theft — the punchline being that a work shirt is an extremely generic garment
jokes.explanation: Roland's perception that a work shirt constitutes a personal style being 'copped' is a good visual gag. The joke is the gap between Roland's panic and the mundanity of the observation. Works as escalation in his paranoia arc.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm going to be spontaneous. I'm going to be sexy.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Okay, that's... that's great.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I just found the inventory spreadsheet. Oh my God, Patrick, look at these numbers. The Q3 projections are... *chef's kiss*. And the office supply closet? Fully stocked. Those gel pens in seventeen different colors...

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David, that's not—

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Don't interrupt me. I'm being spontaneous.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Jeez, I wouldn't mind if he did the bathrooms.'
jokes.setup: Roland is indignant that Johnny might do the bathrooms next
jokes.punchline: Stevie, practical as always, notes she'd actually be fine with that — cutting Roland's outrage off at the knees
jokes.explanation: Stevie's dry practicality is a great counterpoint to Roland's histrionics. The joke works because she doesn't engage with Roland's emotional register at all — she just evaluates the bathroom situation on its merits. Good character integration, strong economy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You know, there's something really sexy about a man who knows how to use... office supplies.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, the stapler is primarily used for binding papers together, and the tape dispenser holds adhesive tape for sealing envelopes and boxes.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, exactly. Very... competent. Keep going.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: The label maker can organize files by creating custom labels, and the hole punch creates uniform holes for two-ring or three-ring binders.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God, stop. I'm getting flustered.

jokes.text: Roland: 'What's gonna happen next? I go to my dermatologist, and Johnny's there getting my moles checked?'
jokes.setup: Johnny says Roland might have to find some other work
jokes.punchline: Roland's paranoia reaches its peak with the image of Johnny at his dermatologist appointment getting his moles checked — the most intimate and specific medical scenario possible
jokes.explanation: The moles are perfect. The escalation from gutter cleaning → strip backgammon → mirror face → moles being checked is a beautifully structured comedic build, and 'getting my moles checked' is the peak because of how specific and intimate it is. This is Roland's funniest moment in the scene.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We should close the store and go on an adventure!

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: We have customers in the store right now.

jokes.text: Patrick walks into the store and finds David doing inventory with rubber thimbles on his fingers. David explains them: 'They help me flip the pages easier.' Patrick: 'Hot. Hot sex.'
jokes.setup: Patrick walks in trying to be romantic and finds David in the least sexy possible work situation
jokes.punchline: Patrick, instead of deflating the moment, fully commits to finding it attractive: 'Hot. Hot sex.'
jokes.explanation: The comedic logic is: the mundane IS romantic between these two. Patrick's complete sincerity in finding rubber thimbles hot is both funny and genuinely sweet — which is the tonal signature of this couple on the show. 'Hot. Hot sex.' as a complete sentence delivered deadpan is very well-constructed.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I need to take the afternoon off. Patrick and I are going to couples counseling.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: We are?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes. Multiple choice: A) We work through our issues, B) We ignore them and resent each other, or C) All of the above?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: That's not how therapy works.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, according to this Groupon, it is.

jokes.text: Patrick: 'Nine to five are our store hours. So it's kinda hard to build customer loyalty when your store is just like, spontaneously open or closed.'
jokes.setup: David suggests they take the afternoon off to mix things up
jokes.punchline: Patrick points out that 'nine to five' is their literal business operating schedule, not a metaphor for routine
jokes.explanation: Patrick's practical correction of David's usage of 'nine to five' is good character comedy. Patrick's responsible-business-partner persona vs. David's impulsivity is a nice dynamic, and the specificity of 'customer loyalty' is a good word choice.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I have an overwhelming urge to destroy something. Preferably something made of plastic and designed to organize.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David, no. We just organized those files.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Your responsibility is suffocating my creativity, Patrick. I need to feel something. Even if that something is the satisfying crunch of a label maker under my heel.

jokes.text: David: 'Yeah, anyway, it turns out Alexis's relationship is in trouble.' / Patrick: 'What?' / David: 'Yeah, I know. Struggling. She practically begged us to join them at the park this afternoon, probably for emotional support.'
jokes.setup: David needs to convince Patrick to leave work early
jokes.punchline: David presents a completely fabricated crisis about Alexis's relationship, framed with straight-faced compassion ('probably for emotional support')
jokes.explanation: David's manipulation here is transparent to the audience but Patrick is taken in. The humor is in David's faux-concern performance and the absurdity of his framing — 'probably for emotional support' is a nice touch of faux-sincerity.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: He's not working because he's productive. He's working because he's avoiding his feelings. It's a classic case of using labor as an emotional anesthetic.

jokes.text: David: 'You know, it's that unshakable sense of responsibility that makes me wanna just... rip those little rubber things, and... burn 'em in a fire.'
jokes.setup: Patrick says they'll just have to start early tomorrow — showing his responsible nature
jokes.punchline: David's 'attraction' to Patrick's responsibility is expressed through the desire to destroy the rubber thimbles that were established as 'hot' earlier
jokes.explanation: Nice callback to the rubber thimbles. The comedic beat works because David's expression of desire for Patrick is always filtered through self-deprecating irony ('burn 'em in a fire'). The pause before 'rip those little rubber things' is doing good structural work.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I haven't had tap water since 1987. I've been on a strict regimen of Evian, San Pellegrino, and the occasional Perrier for palate cleansing. My body is a temple, Johnny. A temple that does not process bacteria.

jokes.text: Roland complaining he couldn't hear about the team lunch 'over the sound of the lawnmower'
jokes.setup: Roland is upset no one told him about the team lunch
jokes.punchline: Stevie explains they tried — but he was drowning them out with the lawnmower he took from Roland
jokes.explanation: Functional callback joke. The lawnmower detail pays off the running gag of Johnny doing Roland's jobs. Not a standout but serves the episode's running thread.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm looking for a Bed or Breakfast, but apparently that's not a thing. I need both the bed AND the breakfast, or I simply cannot function.

jokes.text: Stevie/Roland's attempt to tell Johnny he's working too hard because Moira is away is interrupted by Stevie bluntly finishing Roland's sentence: 'Fill a big, black empty hole in your life.'
jokes.setup: Stevie is gently trying to suggest Johnny is overworking because he misses Moira
jokes.punchline: Roland steamrolls in with the most direct, unvarnished version of what Stevie was diplomatically trying to say
jokes.explanation: The contrast between Stevie's careful phrasing and Roland's blunt 'big black empty hole' is good character comedy. Roland has no social filter, and the gap between what Stevie was building toward and what Roland just says is the joke.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I will not be splitting a bed and breakfast. I'm taking the bed.

jokes.text: 'I didn't bat an eye when I found out my accommodation here was in actuality a small "Bed or Breakfast." They feed me at work, I'll take the bed.'
jokes.setup: Moira insists she's 'never one to complain'
jokes.punchline: The 'Bed or Breakfast' joke — a place so modest it can only offer one of the two — is a clever coinage, followed by her pragmatic resignation: 'I'll take the bed'
jokes.explanation: This is excellent. 'Bed or Breakfast' is a genuinely original joke — a witty inversion of 'Bed and Breakfast' that implies such poverty of amenity that they can only provide one option. The delivery context (while claiming never to complain, while clearly complaining) adds a layer. 'They feed me at work, I'll take the bed' is resigned pragmatism from a woman who once stayed at five-star hotels. Justifies 8+.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, if the director is trying to sink this movie, then I am the figurehead on the bow of this ship, and I will go down with dignity.

jokes.text: Moira: 'This was supposed to be my comeback vehicle, and opportunities like this come along ever so infrequently.' — said about a mutant crow B-movie
jokes.setup: Johnny suggests Moira could just come home if she's not enjoying herself
jokes.punchline: Moira declares this mutant crow project is her comeback vehicle — treating a B-movie as career salvation
jokes.explanation: Moira's unswerving belief in her own importance combined with the gap between her aspirations and the actual project is consistently funny. 'Comeback vehicle' for a mutant crow movie is the central irony of her storyline.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, that video game work is X-rated.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It's not X-rated, it's M for Mature. There's a difference.

jokes.text: Moira: 'This isn't some rinky-dink made-for-TV movie, or a voice gig for an X-rated Japanese video game.' / Moira: 'That-that was rated "M" for "Mature," John.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is trying to inspire Moira about this opportunity
jokes.punchline: The X-rated Japanese video game voice work is revealed as part of Moira's recent career history — and she defensively corrects the rating
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The revelation that Moira has done voice work for a Japanese video game that Johnny calls 'X-rated' is already funny, but her correction — 'That was rated M for Mature, John' — is the perfect finishing move. Her dignity in defending the rating classification while conceding the gig is exactly right for her character. The 'M for Mature' defense is extremely quotable and tells you everything about Moira's self-justifications.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You need to fight for this, Moira. You need to go in there and show them what you're made of.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You're right. I'm going to march in there and I'm going to tell them that I am a serious actress, and I deserve this role.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Besides, look at Jaws 4. That was a terrible sequel, and it still spawned a franchise.

jokes.text: Moira: 'If Sandy Bullock hadn't fought to keep the Speed Franchise alive, we never would've had "Cruise Control".'
jokes.setup: Johnny has inspired Moira to fight for the project; she latches onto a celebrity analogy
jokes.punchline: Moira credits Sandra Bullock for 'fighting to keep the Speed franchise alive' — which is not how Speed 2 happened — and calls it 'Cruise Control' as if that's an inspiring success story (Speed 2 is famously terrible)
jokes.explanation: Excellent joke for film-literate audiences. Speed 2: Cruise Control is famously one of the worst sequels ever made and a disaster for everyone involved — Keanu Reeves refused to return. Moira citing this as an example of career persistence paying off is a perfect encapsulation of her delusion. Calling it just 'Cruise Control' is a nice detail — she only half-remembers the film. The callback to celebrity references being her frame for everything is beautifully integrated.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'll have the soup to go.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Would you like that in a bowl or a cup?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: A bag.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Oh. Um, we have bowls and cups.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Just so you're aware, Mr. Rose, the special is cream of mushroom soup. We don't actually have to-go containers for that, so I've just double-bagged it. Would you like a spoon, or a straw?'
jokes.setup: Johnny has just had a touching phone moment with Moira and is heading back to work
jokes.punchline: Stevie's completely practical revelation that cream of mushroom soup is being transported in double-bagged plastic with a straw option is a perfect tonal gut-punch after the emotional scene
jokes.explanation: Structurally excellent. The emotional beat with Johnny admitting he misses Moira is immediately deflated by the logistics of double-bagged soup. 'Would you like a spoon, or a straw?' is the perfect capper — completely deadpan, utterly practical. This is Stevie's mode perfectly deployed. The visual of cream of mushroom soup in a bag is also inherently funny.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David's afraid of heights.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not afraid of heights. I'm afraid of falling. It's a very different thing. Kafka wrote extensively about the distinction.

jokes.text: Stevie offers Johnny cream of mushroom soup in a double-bagged plastic bag and asks if he wants a spoon or a straw
jokes.setup: Johnny is taking the lunch special to go
jokes.punchline: The restaurant doesn't have to-go containers for soup, so Stevie has double-bagged it in plastic and offers the alternative of a straw — the visual of drinking soup from a bag is the payload
jokes.explanation: The straw option is the punchline — it's the logical conclusion of bagged soup taken to its absurd limit, and Stevie delivers it with complete seriousness. A good visual-comedy beat that plays perfectly in a single-camera format.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Why are you wearing a safety helmet? You look like a tiny construction worker, or like a really large toddler who got into his dad's tool shed.

jokes.text: Alexis to David in a safety helmet: 'David, you look so cute under there! Like a tiny toadstool man, or a little acorn person, or something.'
jokes.setup: David shows up to the adventure ropes course in a safety helmet
jokes.punchline: Alexis's description of him as a 'tiny toadstool man' or 'little acorn person' is simultaneously affectionate and insulting
jokes.explanation: The 'toadstool man / acorn person' descriptions are delightfully specific and affectionate-cruel — a register Alexis excels at. These are not mean-spirited but perfectly observed absurdist images. The 'or something' trailing off is a nice touch of Alexis's casualness.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not a virgin. I lost my virginity on a Heritage trip to Israel when I was sixteen.

jokes.text: Ted: 'It's actually closer to 30 feet, bud.' (correcting the 20-foot estimate of how high the ropes course is)
jokes.setup: David sarcastically remarks on who wouldn't want to dangle 20 feet in the air on shaky logs
jokes.punchline: Ted helpfully corrects it to 30 feet — which makes everything worse — delivered with complete cheerfulness
jokes.explanation: Ted's oblivious cheerfulness — correcting David in a way that only worsens his anxiety — is good character work. The 'bud' is a nice detail of Ted's personality. Simple but effective.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You look flushed. Are you okay?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm fine. It's just the new moisturizer. It's supposed to make you glow, but I think it's giving me an allergic reaction.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David, you're shaking.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, well, the product description did say it provides 'an intense experience.'

jokes.text: Tour guide: 'Uh oh, we've got a virgin!' / David: 'Okay, you might wanna talk to half my birthright trip about that.'
jokes.setup: The tour guide announces David has never done a ropes course before ('we've got a virgin!')
jokes.punchline: David interprets 'virgin' literally and offers his sexual history as a rebuttal — referencing a Birthright trip as the evidence
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection joke. The guide uses 'virgin' in the adventure-sports sense, David weaponizes it as a literal sexual claim. The Birthright trip detail is a specific and funny choice — it's both plausible for David's character and a culturally recognizable enough reference to land. 'Half my birthright trip' implies he's selective about his claim, which is an extra layer.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I need my trailer moved 40 feet to the left. The sun is hitting it at an unflattering angle, and I cannot be expected to prepare for my close-up while looking like a copper penny.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And I'll need Evian water. Room temperature. Not cold. Not room temperature, cold. Lukewarm room temperature.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Also, someone needs to call my aesthetician. This lighting is making my pores look like moon craters, and I refuse to film another scene until this is rectified.

jokes.text: David gets on the ropes course, immediately freezes, and we see him stuck — everyone watching below
jokes.setup: David volunteers to go first with great confidence
jokes.punchline: He immediately freezes on the course and gets stuck, the gap between his bravado and his ability being the joke
jokes.explanation: A visual payoff of the bravado setup. Works in the context of the scene, though as pure physical comedy it's not particularly inventive. The value is in the bravado-to-disaster structure.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I wouldn't say he was fired. I'd say the studio was simply indifferent to his art.

jokes.text: Moira on set, flustered about phone chargers and pressed juice: 'None of my phone chargers are working! I ask for a pressed juice, and all I get are blank stares.'
jokes.setup: Moira is on a B-movie film set that clearly doesn't have the resources of a high-end production
jokes.punchline: Her complaints are the exact entitled star demands that are absurd in this context — pressed juice on a mutant crow movie set
jokes.explanation: Functional character comedy — Moira's demands are clearly wrong-sized for the production. Less sharp than the episode's stronger Moira jokes because it's a more standard 'entitled celebrity' gag without a strong specific twist.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: My character was written out of the show when she fell into a vat of acid at the cosmetics factory, but then she was mysteriously revived as her own twin sister who had been living in a secret underground bunker in Manitoba.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: By season four, I was also a duchess, and had developed a psychic connection to migratory birds.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The writers really committed to the bit.

jokes.text: Moira's soap opera resume: 'I worked in soaps. They had me play my own father, who then became pregnant despite the vasectomy. I still hold the record for the longest-running demonic possession on daytime television.'
jokes.setup: Moira is arguing that every project has potential, using her own career as evidence
jokes.punchline: The evidence of her argument is increasingly surreal soap opera storylines, each more extreme than the last, culminating in 'longest-running demonic possession on daytime television'
jokes.explanation: The episode's best joke and one of the best moments in the season. The escalation is perfectly constructed: playing her own father (already absurd), who then became pregnant (absurdity escalates), despite the vasectomy (precision detail that makes it perfect), and THEN the record for longest demonic possession. Each beat is funnier than the last. The vasectomy is the key word — it makes the pregnancy joke specific and sharp rather than vague. Moira delivers this as genuine evidence of career success, which is the perfect ironic frame. This is anchor-level joke writing.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I was on a soap opera for three years. My character underwent a gender reassignment, became pregnant, and gave birth to twins who were actually her cousins.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'You're reorganizing the shed? That's not on the calendar 'til next week.'
jokes.setup: Johnny reveals he's reorganizing the shed in addition to all his other unsanctioned work
jokes.punchline: Stevie's concern is not emotional but logistical — it's not on the calendar yet
jokes.explanation: Stevie's response frames everything through operational logistics rather than concern — which is funny and character-specific. Works but is minor.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: This coffee tastes like it came from a gutter.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, well, it's still coffee.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'I hope that someday I find someone who I can stand long enough to feel a little lost when they're not around.'
jokes.setup: Stevie is trying to comfort Johnny about missing Moira
jokes.punchline: Her idea of romantic aspiration is finding someone she can merely 'stand' long enough to miss — the bar is on the floor, delivered completely sincerely
jokes.explanation: One of Stevie's best lines in the episode. The joke is also genuinely touching — her romantic hope is framed as the absolute minimum of tolerance, which is both sad and funny. 'Someone who I can stand long enough to feel a little lost when they're not around' is beautifully constructed. It earns its emotional resonance while being funny. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Love is when someone gets annoyed with you on a more regular basis than with other people.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Eight days, Stevie. That's the longest we've gone without seeing each other. No matter where we were, and... yeah, we've managed to keep it up for almost 40 years.'
jokes.setup: Stevie encourages Johnny to admit he misses Moira
jokes.punchline: Johnny's admission that 8 days is their all-time record — maintained across 40 years — is both warm and quietly absurd. The implication that even while they were wealthy jet-setters, they saw each other daily is a sweet but gently funny detail.
jokes.explanation: This is warmer than funny, but the comedy is in the specificity of '8 days' and 40 years — a quietly absurd detail about a very devoted couple. Primarily serves the emotional throughline but has a comedic undertone.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm struggling because... well, because I've built my entire life around Moira. And when you do that, when you center everything around another person, you lose yourself.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Johnny...

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, let me finish. The truth is, I don't want to find myself. I like being lost with you.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'I'll tell you what's not out of sorts anymore, that shed!'
jokes.setup: Stevie has just helped Johnny emotionally acknowledge missing Moira — a tender moment
jokes.punchline: Johnny pivots immediately to boasting about the shed he reorganized — emotional deflection via enthusiastic housekeeping
jokes.explanation: The tonal pivot from emotional vulnerability to shed pride is a perfect Johnny Rose beat. The exclamation point energy on 'that shed!' is funny — he's genuinely more comfortable talking about the shed than his feelings. Good character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, David, you're doing great! Just keep going!

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Yeah, you've almost got it. Don't worry, we're rooting for you.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Seriously though, this is such a... *rope* situation.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Did you just make a pun while he's literally struggling?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, I'm just trying to help him *tie* one on... mentally.

jokes.text: Tour guide: 'You're looking pretty shaky there, David. I really hope we didn't "rope" you into something here.'
jokes.setup: David is visibly struggling on the ropes course
jokes.punchline: The guide makes a pun — 'rope you into something' — while David is literally on a rope, scared
jokes.explanation: The pun is extremely obvious — almost too obvious for this show. 'Rope you into something' is the most predictable adventure-guide joke possible. It gets a minor groan/smile at best. The show is usually smarter than this. The guide saying the pun with quote fingers around 'rope' makes it even more labored.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David is literally afraid of heights. Like, when we were kids, he wouldn't even go on the second step of a ladder.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I have a very legitimate fear. When I was seven, I fell off the monkey bars at camp, and a counselor told me I looked like Anderson Cooper when I was crying.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: That's not a fear of heights, David.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It is a very real fear of humiliation.

jokes.text: Ted: 'Hey David, just pretend you're in like, a super dangerous walk-and-turn sobriety test.'
jokes.setup: David is struggling on the ropes course and people below are trying to encourage him
jokes.punchline: Ted's suggested visualization is a sobriety test — implying David has experience with those specifically
jokes.explanation: The sobriety test callback to David's past is a nice specific dig — it's an oddly specific choice from Ted, implying he knows enough about David's past to connect ropes courses to drunk tests. Ted's chirpy helpfulness making things worse is good character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So you rigged the quiz the whole time?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, yes, obviously. I mean, did you really think I knew the answers to those questions?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't know what to believe anymore, Alexis.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, David, it was just a little quiz. I was trying to help you feel better about yourself.

jokes.text: Alexis reveals David was broken up with while parasailing in the Seychelles, stuck up there with Anderson Cooper for three hours until the wind died down
jokes.setup: Alexis is explaining why David is afraid of heights — 'something to do with him being broken up with while he was...'
jokes.punchline: The circumstances are: (1) parasailing, (2) in the Seychelles, (3) with Anderson Cooper, (4) stuck for three hours, (5) until the wind died down. Each detail escalates the absurdity.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's two or three best moments. The Anderson Cooper parasailing story is a textbook escalating absurdist backstory. Each detail is funnier than the last: 'Seychelles' sets the opulence, 'Anderson Cooper' is the perfect celebrity choice (specific, vaguely plausible for David's world, inherently funny name to be stuck with), 'three hours' gives it duration and therefore suffering, and 'until the wind died down' is the deflating practical detail that makes it real. This is exceptional comedic writing — high originality, perfect structure, completely character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm taking this relationship quiz seriously. It's important to understand our compatibility.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, David, the quiz is completely rigged. It's designed to give you bad results no matter what you answer.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What? Why would you let me waste my time on a rigged quiz?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Because it's funny?

jokes.text: Alexis: 'I totally forgot! Argh! I'm sorry, David!' — having just revealed the Anderson Cooper story that she organized this entire trip forgetting about
jokes.setup: Alexis suddenly remembers why David is afraid of heights — after organizing a heights-based adventure
jokes.punchline: Her dismay is genuine but also darkly funny — she created the entire situation by forgetting the very reason he couldn't do this
jokes.explanation: Solid character beat — Alexis's self-absorbed planning forgot the one thing that would make this terrible for her brother. The reaction is earned but the laugh is mostly carried by the Anderson Cooper revelation before it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I love the simple things in life. A perfectly organized closet. A well-made suit. A rubber thimble.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: But you know what I don't love? Rubber thimbles. They're awful.

jokes.text: David: 'I'm sorry, you were right, the quiz was rigged.' / 'What?! What quiz?'
jokes.setup: Multiple revelations are happening at once — Alexis's guilt about the Anderson Cooper story, and David stuck on the course
jokes.punchline: David's terrified apology about the quiz he took circles back as everyone's confusion escalates
jokes.explanation: Functional callback that resolves the quiz subplot during a moment of physical crisis — the comedic escalation of emotional and physical peril together is a good structural choice.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: This crow is not just a bird—it's a symbol of rebirth, of transformation, of the indomitable human spirit trapped in feathered form. And I will not rest until the world sees what I see: a masterpiece of avian cinema that will make Hitchcock weep into his martini. *caws and flaps arms* We're not just making a movie, we're making a statement. A statement that says: 'This cast of characters will do absolutely anything.' And that's a real *raven* of truth.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'David, I lied! I took the quiz like, 15 times. "In Need of a Generator" is like, the only answer you can get.'
jokes.setup: The whole adventure was organized because David felt bad about the quiz result
jokes.punchline: Alexis admits she knew all along the quiz was rigged — she took it 15 times herself and the bad result is all you can get
jokes.explanation: A well-earned structural callback. Alexis took it 15 times herself, mirroring David's behavior exactly. The reversal — she was using the quiz as a weapon knowing it was rigged — is satisfying both emotionally and comedically. The parallel structure to David's own 'it's rigged' claim is neat. This is good episode architecture.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: So these digital birds, are they rendering in real-time or are they pre-rendered assets? And what's the poly count on the feathers? I need to know if they're going to compete with my eyeline.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Also, what's their behavioral algorithm? Because if they're going to move unpredictably, I'm going to need to adjust my sightline, and frankly, I've already committed to a very specific focal point for this scene.

jokes.text: Patrick from the ground: 'I don't know what this is about, but this generator is running smoothly.'
jokes.setup: The quiz-rigged revelation is happening while David is stuck 30 feet up
jokes.punchline: Patrick, who never understood the quiz metaphor to begin with, deploys it confidently and correctly — asserting his relationship is fine in the exact language David was taught
jokes.explanation: Good callback and character moment. Patrick previously said he didn't understand the generator metaphor, but he has picked it up and uses it with perfect confidence and timing. His 'I don't know what this is about, but...' makes it even better — he's still slightly unsure but commits anyway. Warm, funny, and a nice payoff for the couple's storyline.

jokes.text: Patrick: 'David, you're basically 40 years old, I shouldn't be able to make you feel bad about anything.'
jokes.setup: David admits he let Alexis make him feel bad about the quiz
jokes.punchline: Patrick's response is a perfectly loving but also mildly brutal reality check — 'basically 40' is doing a lot of work
jokes.explanation: 'Basically 40' is the comedic knife in an otherwise warm statement. Patrick is right and loving, but 'basically 40' is also just a mild dig that David would absolutely register. The combination of support and gentle mockery is exactly right for this couple.

jokes.text: Patrick's speech: 'I love our relationship. I love it when you order me pizza. I love when you use words like inventory! I even love those stupid rubber things you put on your fingers, 'cause you think they flip the pages faster. They don't flip the pages faster. They don't flip the pages faster!'
jokes.setup: Patrick is trying to reassure David their relationship is great
jokes.punchline: His declaration of love escalates from genuinely sweet (pizza) to absurd (the word 'inventory') to the thimbles — and ends with a repeated insistence 'They don't flip the pages faster' that's both funny and strangely passionate
jokes.explanation: The episode's emotional and comedic apex, and the best callback. The speech works on every level: it's genuinely sweet (Patrick catalogues the specific mundane things he loves), it's funny (the escalation to 'inventory' as a love-word), and it pays off multiple earlier callbacks (the thimbles, the inventory day). 'They don't flip the pages faster. They don't flip the pages faster!' — the repetition and urgency is both absurd and passionate. This is exceptional character comedy that also advances the emotional story. The kind of joke that makes an audience laugh and go 'aww' simultaneously.

jokes.text: Patrick's rope course speech: 'I love it when you order me pizza. I love when you use words like inventory! I even love those stupid rubber things you put on your fingers, 'cause you think they flip the pages faster. They don't flip the pages faster. They don't flip the pages faster!'
jokes.setup: David has admitted to faking excitement to prove their relationship has spark
jokes.punchline: Patrick's declaration of love is built entirely from the mundane domestic details of their life — pizza orders, the word 'inventory,' rubber thimbles — with the thimble denial delivered twice for escalating emphasis
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's emotional and comedic peak. Patrick's love letter is constructed entirely from callbacks to earlier jokes — pizza, inventory, rubber thimbles — which means it only works because the episode earned it. The repeated 'They don't flip the pages faster!' is the escalating insistence of someone who has thought about this a lot. The comedy and the love are completely intertwined. This is Schitt's Creek at its best: deeply funny and genuinely moving simultaneously. Justifies 8+ clearly.

jokes.text: Moira's on-set monologue: 'My dear murder, soon we will once more walk amongst the humans. But until that day comes, we must remember, the crows don't just have eyes... we also have wings! Caaaaw! Awk! Caw! Caw!'
jokes.setup: Moira is now fully committed to the mutant crow movie, delivering a big motivational speech to the other crow characters
jokes.punchline: She ends the speech with full crow sound effects — 'Caaaaw! Awk! Caw! Caw!' — performed with total earnestness and conviction
jokes.explanation: The payoff of Moira's entire storyline. She went from dismissing this as a dumpster fire to fully embodying a crow character — and doing it with complete Method commitment. 'My dear murder' (using the collective noun for crows) is a sophisticated choice. The crow sounds at the end, delivered with full theatrical investment, are the visual/audio punchline the whole episode has been building to. This is exceptional character comedy.

jokes.text: Moira's final on-screen speech: 'I am but a tailfeather away from finding the cure. So please... quiet your caws so that we may take up our cause: redemption!'
jokes.setup: Moira delivers her in-movie speech as the mutant crow scientist
jokes.punchline: 'quiet your caws so that we may take up our cause' — an unscripted(?)-feeling internal rhyme that is both absurd as crow-movie dialogue and impressive as a poetic coincidence
jokes.explanation: 'Caws' and 'cause' is a homophone pun that Moira delivers with complete dramatic commitment — it works as crow-movie dialogue AND as unintentional wordplay. 'I am but a tailfeather away from finding the cure' is also an inherently funny sentence because of how earnest it is about such a ridiculous premise. The whole speech is a comedic triumph of commitment over content.

tmdb_overview: While Moira is in Bosnia filming the Crows movie that she hopes will revive her acting career, David attempts to take his relationship with Patrick to new heights.
insight: Character-driven absurdism sustains 83.8 score with 2.33 jokes per minute.
